next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

The blast came downriver before the sun had burned the rain out of the weeds. It ran through the crockery in Della's cupboard, through the nailheads in the wall, and then through her own ribs while she climbed the muddy path to Old Sion with an oilcloth folded under her arm. The rain from the night before still hung in the grass. Water dropped from cedar boughs in slow cold taps against her sleeves. Every few breaths the valley gave back another far metal report from the Marlowe works, and each one seemed to shove a little more of Brier Valley toward the river.

Somebody below the graveyard fence called for her before she reached the gate.

"Miz Della."

It was one of the county boys, his voice gone thin with the kind of alarm men used when they had already put their hands where they should not have. She crossed through the wet grass between the limestone markers and saw at once that the trouble was not in the tagged rows Lucille Holt had been keeping neat with her string and stakes. It was lower. Fever ground.

The bank there had peeled open in the night. Rain had come hard on the slope after yesterday's blasting, and the red clay had slumped off in a long raw crescent, carrying broom sedge and loose rock with it. Under the torn face of the hill a pine box had shouldered halfway out of the earth. One side plank showed wet and dark as tobacco spit. At the downhill end the wood had sprung apart just enough to show black cloth and the curled leather edge of a shoe sole.

Della stopped so hard mud slid over the tops of her shoes.

Lucille Holt stood two yards back...
open_hooks_to_carry: The Bledsoe household's economic strain is rendered through food, repairs, and unpaid obligations.
June notices that Della is hiding papers and starts paying closer attention.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...clay from the path. At the turn he stooped and pulled the tomato stake free from his belt, then carried it in his hand the rest of the way downhill as if he had remembered, too late, what he had really meant to spend the morning doing.

Lucille stood beside the pump in silence for a moment. The schoolhouse behind them had begun swallowing voices again, one argument ended and three more starting up in its place.

"I can come by the house if Thursday seems easier," she said finally.

"No."

Lucille inclined her head. She knew when not to offer softness. "Nine o'clock, then."

She went back inside. Della remained at the pump with water dripping from her fingertips and drying red on the concrete below. Across the cemetery, yellow tags had begun to show here and there against stone and dark wood, small squares kicking in the breeze. On the upper row they looked harmless enough. Just paper. Just twine. Just a clerk's way of not losing count. But Sadie's grave had already proved what those tags could do once a surname touched them.

Below the cedar shade, where the older ground sloped toward the fever plot and the markers thinned into absence, the hillside looked no different from any other patch of worn earth. That was the worst of it. Dirt kept its face no matter what was under it. A stranger could stand there and see only work. Della stood with the smell of opened grave still in her apron and understood what the county had bought for four dollars a day. Not only her back. Not only her hands. They had bought the part of her that remembered which dead could still cost the living.
state_deltas_required: Decide how much of her private record Della can risk bringing back into use.
Della reactivates the ledger, which gives her leverage but also makes her vulnerable to discovery and theft.
